perturbation.original_text: Either party may terminate this Agreement without cause, by giving the other party five (30) Business Days advance written notice.
perturbation.changed_text: Either party may terminate this Agreement without cause, by giving the other party one (1) Business Days advance written notice.
perturbation.explanation: Changing the termination notice period from 30 business days to 1 business day introduces a legal contradiction, as Korean law may require a longer notice period for contract termination.
perturbation.law_explanation: Article 660 of the Korean Civil Act stipulates notice periods for termination of contracts. While it depends on the type of contract, unilaterally reducing the notice period to 1 business day may violate the reasonable notice requirements implied under Korean contract law, particularly if significant investments or reliance are involved. Specific contract types will have explicit notice periods.

perturbation.justification: The changed text reduces the termination notice period from 30 business days to 1 business day. The Law Explanation indicates that Article 660 of the Korean Civil Act specifies notice periods for contract termination and that reducing the notice period could violate reasonable notice requirements, particularly if significant investments are involved. The Perturbation Explanation supports this by stating Korean law may require a longer notice period. The original text provided 30 business days while the changed text modifies this to one day which creates an inconsistency with the legally mandated time frame. Snippet 1 is not directly relevant to the legal issue but does highlight that Korean laws are involved.

perturbation.original_text: Each party shall immediately upon the Termination of this Agreement or at any time upon the request of the Disclosing Party, discontinue use of the Confidential Information of the other and, if requested by the Disclosing Party, return same and all copies thereof which may be or have been in such party’s direct or indirect possession or control. If return is not requested, the Confidential Information shall be destroyed within ten (10) Business Days of the Termination of the Agreement and an officer’s certificate to that effect provided by the Disclosing Party.
perturbation.changed_text: Each party shall immediately upon the Termination of this Agreement or at any time upon the request of the Disclosing Party, discontinue use of the Confidential Information of the other and, if requested by the Disclosing Party, return same and all copies thereof which may be or have been in such party’s direct or indirect possession or control. If return is not requested, the Confidential Information shall be destroyed within one hundred eighty (180) Business Days of the Termination of the Agreement and an officer’s certificate to that effect provided by the Disclosing Party.
perturbation.explanation: Extending the destruction period to 180 business days may violate data protection laws or regulations in Korea, which could require more prompt destruction of confidential information upon termination of an agreement to reduce the risk of unauthorized disclosure or misuse.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Personal Information Protection Act (PIPA) of South Korea
perturbation.law_explanation: Article 21 of the PIPA mandates that personal information must be destroyed without delay when it becomes unnecessary, such as after contract termination. Delaying destruction for 180 business days could be considered a violation if the information is no longer needed and exposes it to potential risks.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: rn the collection, use, and disclosure of personal health information by health information custodians. To provide patients with a right to request access to and correction of their records of personal health information held by health information custodians. To impose administrative requirements (regulations) on custodians with respect to records of personal health information. Amendment [ edit ] On June 18, 2015, the Digital Privacy Act (Senate Bill S-4 [ 18 ] ) became law, amending the PIPEDA [ 19 ] to include a business transaction exemption, mandatory breach notification requirements, enhanced powers for the Privacy Commissioner, and various other updates. The PIPEDA sets out ground rules for how private sector organizations may collect, use or disclose personal information in the course of commercial activities. PIPEDA also applies to federal works, undertakings, and business in respect of employee personal information. The law gives individuals the right to access and request correction of the personal information these organizations may have collected about them. In general, PIPEDA applies to organizations' commercial activities in all provinces, except organizations that collect, use or disclose personal information entirely within provinces that have their own privacy laws, which have been declared substantially similar to the federal law. In such cases, it is the substantially similar provincial law that will apply instead of PIPEDA, although PIPEDA continues to apply to federal works, undertakings or businesses and to interprovincial or international transfers of personal information. Recently, the Office of the Privacy Commissioner of Canada, as well as academics and members of civil society, have claimed that it does not address modern challenges of privacy law sufficiently, calling for reforming PIPEDA in view of AI. [ 20 ] The Canadian government responded to these calls with a comprehensive reform project currently under Parliamentary discussion. [ 21 ] Remedies [ edit ] The Act does not create an automatic right to sue for violations of the law's obligations. Instead, PIPEDA follows an ombudsman model in which complaints are taken to the Office of the Privacy Commissioner of Canada . The Commissioner is required to investigate the complaint and to produce a report at its conclusion. The report is not binding on the parties but is more of a recommendation. The Commissioner does not have any powers to order compliance, award damages, or levy penalties. The organization complained about does not have to follow the recommendations. The complainant, with the report in hand, can then take the matter to the Federal Court of Canada . The responding organization cannot take the matter to the courts, because the report is not a decision and PIPEDA does not explicitly grant the responding organization the right to do so. [ citation needed ] PIPEDA provides, at section 14, the complainant the right to apply to the Federal Court of Canada for a hearing with respect to the subject matter of the complaint. The Court has the power to order the organization to correct its practices, to publicize the steps it will take to correct its practices, and to award damages. [ 22 ] Proposed Bill C-475 [ edit ] As a result of long-enduring and central gap in Canada's privacy protections, [ 23 ] Bill C-475 was proposed in February 2013 by Charmaine Borg, MP, proposing several amendments to the Act . [ 24 ] Bill C-475 was defeated in January 2014. [ 25 ] See also [ edit ] Fighting Internet and Wireless Spam Act Information privacy law Information privacy References [ edit ] ^ "Personal Information Protection and Electronic Documents Act" (PDF) . Archived from the original (PDF) on 2011-04-01. ^ McClennan, Jennifer P.; Schick, Vadim (2007). "O, Privacy: Canada's Importance in the Development of the International Data Privacy Regime". Georgetown Journal of International Law . 38 : 669– 693. ^ Section 29 of the Act ^ PIPEDA Review – Privacy Co
perturbation.justification: The modification extends the period for destroying confidential information from 10 to 180 business days. The Law Explanation states Article 21 of PIPA mandates that personal information must be destroyed without delay when it becomes unnecessary, such as after contract termination. While Scraped Law Snippets 1 provides no direct support, the Law Explanation states that delaying destruction for 180 business days could be considered a violation if the information is no longer needed and exposes it to potential risks. This aligns with the Inconsistencies Legal Contradiction definition: "when a contract sets a deadline or requirement that violates federal or state law, making the contractual terms unenforceable or illegal."

perturbation.original_text: Any notice which may or is required to be given pursuant to this Agreement shall be in writing and shall be sufficiently given or made if delivered by facsimile, by overnight courier or by certified or registered mail, return receipt requested, and shall be effective (a) upon receipt if delivered personally; (b) on the business day the notice is received by facsimile; (c) one business day after being sent by overnight courier; and (d) three (3) business days after being deposited in mail, postage prepaid.
perturbation.changed_text: Any notice which may or is required to be given pursuant to this Agreement shall be in writing and shall be sufficiently given or made if delivered by standard mail, and shall be effective (a) thirty (30) business days after being deposited in mail, postage prepaid.
perturbation.explanation: Changing the notice method to only standard mail and making it effective 30 business days after mailing could conflict with Korean law if it is deemed an unreasonably long delay for time-sensitive communications. Korean courts might find this insufficient if it prejudices one party.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Korean Civil Act, General Principles of Contract Law
perturbation.law_explanation: While the Korean Civil Act doesn't specify methods of notice, general principles require fairness and reasonableness. A 30-day delay via standard mail, especially when faster methods exist, could be challenged as unreasonable if it causes detriment or violates implicit duties of good faith.

perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: Civil Code of the Republic of Korea - Wikipedia Jump to content From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia South Korean legislation This article needs additional citations for verification . Please help improve this article by adding citations to reliable sources . Unsourced material may be challenged and removed. Find sources: "Civil Code of the Republic of Korea" – news · newspapers · books · scholar · JSTOR ( November 2024 ) ( Learn how and when to remove this message ) The Civil Code of the Republic of Korea ( South Korea ) was passed in 1958 as Law No. 471 and is known in South Korea as one of the three fundamental laws, the other two being Criminal law and constitution . It is made up of five parts, Part I (general provisions), Part II (real rights), Part III (claims), Part IV (relatives), and Part V (inheritance). [ 1 ] History [ edit ] The South Korean Civil Code is the largest code among South Korean law. During the period of Japanese rule (1910-1945), Japanese civil code was used, but family law and succession law partially followed Korean customary rules. After the establishment of the South Korean government, the Committee of Law Compilation (법률편찬위원회) proceed to legislate civil code and other codes in 1948 and completed in 1953. The South Korean government supplied the draft to the legislature in 1954, which then passed the civil code into law in 1957 after some amendments, mostly relating to the family law. After it was enacted, the South Korean civil code has been modified seven times. Especially in family law, patriarchal system of family law was abolished in order to achieve the equality of the sexes in 1990. [ citation needed ] For example, in the area of child custody, both parents came to have equal rights, and the household registration system (호주제, hojuje ) was abolished as unconstitutional. Contents [ edit ] Part 1: General Provisions [ edit ] General provisions (민법총칙) is a basic principle applying to the substantive law, according to the "Pandekten system". In the section of source of law, customary law and sound reasoning are also considered as source of law (Article 1). "Trust and good faith rule" is also stated in article 2. General provisions consists of seven chapters: Common provisions (source of law) Persons capacity domicile absence and disappearance Juristic persons incorporation organization dissolution penal provisions Things Juristic acts general provision declaration of intention agency nullity and voidance conditions and time Period Extinctive prescription Part 2: Property Rights [ edit ] Property rights (물권법) are influenced by Roman law and German law, but the "right to registered lease on deposit basis" (chonsegwon) has the most strong indigenous features. According to article 303, any person having chonsegwon is entitled to use it in conformity with its purposes and to take the profits from it by paying the deposit money and possessing the real property owned by another person. In customary rule, customary superficies, right of grave base are also applied except nine rights written in the real rights part. In real estate, effect of changes in real rights is only valid by registration. This is because the rights and relations of real estate can be clear by the registration which serves the security of real estate contract. Real rights consists of General Provisions (chapters 1 - 3) and Servitude (chapters 4 - 7): Possessory right Ownership (limitation of ownership, acquisition of ownership, joint ownership) Superficies Right to registered lease on deposit basis, ‘ Chonsegwon ’, Right of retention, Pledge pledge of movables pledge of rights Mortgage Part 3: Claims [ edit ] Claims (채권법) consists of contract, management of affairs, unjust enrichment and tort. Contract part covers formation of contract, offer and acceptance and the kinds of contracts, gift sale and exchange, etc. In tort part, torts and damages are defined. Korean definition of torts is stated in article 750; "any person who cause
perturbation.justification: The original text provided multiple methods for delivering notice, including overnight courier and facsimile. The changed text limits this to standard mail and significantly extends the effective delivery time to 30 business days. The Perturbation Explanation notes that this delay could be unreasonable under Korean law. The Law Explanation reinforces this, stating that even though Korean Civil Act doesn't explicitly specify notice methods, it requires fairness and reasonableness. A 30-day delay via standard mail is potentially unreasonable and detrimental, violating implicit duties of good faith. This violates the Inconsistencies - Legal Contradiction definition, as a time-sensitive obligation is not aligned with legal requirements and could render the contractual terms unenforceable or illegal.

perturbation.original_text: And it shall hold good for two years (730 days) starting when both sides sign.
perturbation.changed_text: And it shall hold good indefinitely starting when both sides sign.
perturbation.explanation: Changing the contract term to 'indefinitely' may be problematic under Korean law, as perpetual contracts are generally disfavored or require specific conditions for enforceability.
perturbation.law_explanation: Article 658 of the Korean Civil Act addresses contracts for continuous performance. While not explicitly prohibiting indefinite terms, it implies a need for conditions allowing termination and may be interpreted to require a reasonable basis for contracts of such duration. Simply stating 'indefinitely' could render the term unenforceable without such provisions.

perturbation.justification: The change from a definite two-year term to an 'indefinitely' term creates a potential legal contradiction. The Law Explanation states that Korean Civil Act Article 658 implies a need for termination conditions and a reasonable basis for indefinite contracts, suggesting that simply stating 'indefinitely' could render the term unenforceable. While Scraped Law Snippet 1 regarding SMEs is not directly relevant, the Perturbation Explanation states that perpetual contracts are generally disfavored under Korean law, supporting the claim that changing the term to 'indefinitely' introduces a legal contradiction, as it deviates from standard contract practices and could be challenged in court.
